title: Channel-Wise Mixing Without Spatial Mixing
concept: A 1x1 convolution mixes information across channels at each pixel independently, without ever looking at neighboring pixels — spatial layout is left completely untouched.

exercises.prompt: Recap: A 1x1 convolution filter at one pixel position looks at...

options.text: Every input channel at that pixel, blended into one number
A 3x3 patch of neighboring pixels in a single channel

exercises.explain: Right — full channel depth, zero neighbors. Now let's see exactly what that blending is really doing.

cards.title: 64 measurements, stacked on one point
cards.body: Imagine a single pixel carrying 64 different measurements about itself — one channel screaming 'edge!', another whispering 'redness', another tracking 'furriness'. What if you could blend all 64 into a smarter combined signal, without ever glancing at the pixel next door?

cards.title: Channel-wise mixing, pixel by pixel
cards.body: A 1x1 convolution performs channel-wise mixing: at each pixel position, independently, it takes the stack of channel values right there and computes a weighted combination of them. It repeats the exact same recipe at every position — mixing across channels, never across space.

cards.title: A blender that never leaves its cup
cards.body: Think of each pixel position as its own smoothie cup holding several ingredients (channels). A 1x1 conv is a blender that mixes the ingredients inside ONE cup using a fixed recipe — but it never pours anything into the cup next to it. Every cup gets blended on its own, with the same recipe.

cards.title: Two recipes, two new channels
cards.body: One pixel has 3 channels: Edge=8, Color=3, Texture=6. Filter A has weights [1, 0, 1] → 8 + 0 + 6 = 14 (combines edge + texture, ignores color). Filter B has weights [0, 1, 0] → 3 (isolates color only). Stack both outputs and that same pixel now has 2 new channels: [14, 3] — same position, brand-new channel stack.

cards.title: Not the same as blurring or resizing
cards.body: It's easy to lump 1x1 convs in with other filters that shrink an image. But a 1x1 conv never touches height or width — the spatial grid is preserved pixel-for-pixel. Only the channel depth at each position can change, not the size of the grid itself.

cards.title: Spatial mixing vs. channel mixing
cards.body: Two very different kinds of 'blending' inside a CNN:

right.text:
Blends channels together
at the same pixel,
grid untouched

exercises.prompt: A 1x1 conv layer with 10 output filters is applied to a 64-channel feature map. How many channels does the output have?
exercises.explain: Correct — each of the 10 filters produces one output channel, regardless of how many input channels it read from.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a 1x1 convolution changes the height and width of a feature map.
exercises.explain: False — a 1x1 conv (stride 1, no padding needed) leaves height and width exactly as they were. Only channel depth can change.

exercises.sentence: A 1x1 convolution mixes information across ___, while leaving the spatial grid exactly as it was.
exercises.explain: Right — channels get blended, but the width and height grid never moves.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes what a 1x1 convolution's 'mixing' actually does?

options.text: Combines the channel values at one pixel into new channel values, at that same pixel
Averages a pixel with its 8 surrounding neighbors
Shrinks the image down to half its width and height

exercises.explain: Exactly — mixing happens across the channel stack, position by position, never across neighboring pixels.

exercises.prompt: One pixel has 5 channel values: [10, 0, 5, 20, -5]. You want a single 1x1 filter that passes through ONLY channel 4 (value 20) and ignores every other channel completely. Which weight vector does that?
exercises.explain: Correct: weights [0,0,0,1,0] zero out every channel except the 4th, which passes through unchanged: (10×0)+(0×0)+(5×0)+(20×1)+(-5×0) = 20. Channel mixing can isolate a channel, blend several, or anything in between. Next: using this to shrink channel depth on purpose.
